stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • The 1927 Solvay Conference on Physics gathered twenty-nine leading scientists to discuss quantum theory.
  • The conference featured a now-famous debate between Werner Heisenberg and Erwin Schrödinger.
  • Heisenberg argued that the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known.
  • Schrödinger pushed back, arguing that a complete wave description preserved a more deterministic physical picture.
  • Heisenberg's interpretation eventually became the more widely held view.
stem: The student wants to place Schrödinger's argument within its historical context. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: At the 1927 Solvay Conference on Physics, Schrödinger disagreed with Heisenberg's argument that position and momentum cannot both be precisely known.
The attendees of the 1927 Solvay Conference were among the preeminent scientists of their era, including Schrödinger, who opposed Heisenberg's proposal.
In 1927, Heisenberg and Schrödinger engaged in a famous debate; Heisenberg's argument would remain widely held decades later.
During the formative years of quantum theory, Schrödinger argued for a more deterministic physical picture, although Heisenberg's opposing interpretation would become the more widely held view.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer because it situates Schrödinger's argument both historically (during the formative years of quantum theory) and in relation to the interpretation that ultimately prevailed.

Choices A and B describe the disagreement without giving the broader historical context. Choice C focuses on the debate but doesn't place Schrödinger's argument in context.
